topic: barrel ageing and oak flavour integration

steps.label: cause
steps.text: Wine is aged in oak barrels (new or old, various sizes) for a period measured in months to years

steps.label: mechanism
steps.text: Oxygen enters slowly through barrel staves, softening tannins and stabilising colour; flavour compounds (vanillin, lactones, toast compounds) are extracted from the oak wood; lees contact (if present) adds texture

steps.label: effect
steps.text: The wine shows vanilla, toast, spice, and coconut flavours from the oak; tannins are softened and integrated; colour is stabilised; the wine develops additional structural complexity

steps.label: exam_formulation
steps.text: Barrel ageing imparts vanilla and toast flavours from oak and allows slow oxygen ingress, softening tannins and adding structural complexity.

sat_relevance: Oak-derived characteristics (vanilla, toast, coconut) are assessed on the nose and palate. New oak and barrel ageing are significant winemaking decisions that affect style, intensity, complexity, and ageing potential.
